title: Ecological Succession Timeline
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Animated timeline comparing primary and secondary succession, showing landscape changes, soil development, and biodiversity over time

learningObjectives: Compare primary and secondary succession by manipulating a timeline that shows community changes over time
Explain why secondary succession proceeds faster than primary succession
Analyze the relationship between succession stage, soil development, and biodiversity
prerequisites: Understanding of biodiversity as a measure of ecosystem health

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Interactive timeline exploration with togglable comparison
keyQuestions: What is the key difference between how primary and secondary succession begin?
Why does soil development matter so much for the pace of succession?
How do pioneer species change the environment to make it suitable for later species?
What happens to biodiversity during succession, and why?
How do disturbances reset or alter the succession process?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think succession always leads to a single fixed climax community, when in reality disturbances create a mosaic of successional stages
Students may believe that primary and secondary succession involve completely different species, rather than the same general sequence at different starting points
Students sometimes think succession is a fast process, not realizing primary succession can take centuries to millennia
assessmentOpportunities: Have students create comparative data tables for primary vs. secondary succession at matched stages
Ask students to predict succession outcomes after different types of disturbance
Students draw and label their own succession timeline for a local ecosystem
